The way I keep explaining it, internally and to customers, goes back to why Akamai exists. We were founded in 1998 to solve a delivery problem: content from a handful of central data centres could not reach users fast enough, so we moved it to the edge, close to the user. That became the CDN. Inference is hitting the same wall, and the same architecture answers it. Akamai Inference Cloud runs models at the edge, close to where customers are. We built it in partnership with NVIDIA, pairing our globally distributed platform with NVIDIA Blackwell AI infrastructure so that accelerated compute sits at the edge, close to your customers rather than locked in a central region. The logic of the partnership is simple: NVIDIA brings the AI compute, we bring the reach, and together that puts real-time inference everywhere your customers are.
...
What has shifted the tone lately is the proof. Akamai recently signed the largest deal in its history, a seven-year, $1.8 billion agreement with a leading frontier model provider, winning against hyperscalers and neoclouds based on scale, performance, reliability, and low latency. When a customer asks whether the platform can carry serious AI load, that is a useful place to start.

Akamai's answer is Firewall for AI, purpose-built to protect LLM applications and the APIs behind them. It inspects both inbound prompts and outbound responses in real time, which is what lets it cover threats from both directions. On the external side, it detects and blocks prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, adversarial inputs, model scraping and extraction, and the AI-specific denial-of-service patterns designed to flood a model and exhaust it.
...
The internal risk is the one customers often underestimate: the model leaking sensitive or personal data in its replies, or producing toxic, biased, or non-compliant output that turns into a reputational and regulatory problem. Firewall for AI applies guardrails on the response side to catch exactly that before it reaches the user. Because it runs on the same Akamai edge that retail and travel teams already use for delivery and application security, it deploys inline or through an API without re-architecting anything, and it maps to the governance frameworks customers are now being asked about, including the OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST, and the EU AI Act.
...
That last point is exactly why Akamai's approach holds up against a frontier model such as Anthropic's Claude Mythos. Rather than chasing one brand-name model, Akamai solves for the general case of AI-driven automation, so the same defence-in-depth remains effective if attackers switch from Mythos to another frontier model. In practice, that means identifying headless-browser frameworks despite spoofing during reconnaissance, using behavioural analytics to catch abnormal probing before an exploit runs, shielding assets with adaptive, AI-powered WAF detections when machine-speed exploits slip past static rules, and constraining lateral movement with segmentation so a single breach cannot spread.
...
The AI revolution in commerce isn't just about deploying the smartest models; it's about doing so securely, efficiently, and as close to the end-user as possible. As the threat landscape and customer expectations continue to evolve, our approach must shift from centralised and reactive to edge-native and proactive.
